title: Why the best recommendation lists feel personal rather than authoritative
excerpt: A short essay on curation, trust, and why people return to individuals with taste instead of faceless lists.
body: The appeal of a strong recommendation list is not objectivity. It is personality under discipline. A good list does not pretend to be neutral; it reveals a sensibility worth trusting.
That is why I would rather read a page assembled by someone with real preferences than a polished, committee-safe ranking designed not to offend anyone.
This site is meant to lean into that. The archive matters, but the taste behind the archive matters more.

comments.author: Marian
comments.text: This feels owned in a way most recommendation sites do not.

comments.author: Julian
comments.text: The distinction between archive and taste is exactly right.

title: On building a site that becomes a memory palace for your interests
excerpt: Part essay, part design note: how tags, notes, and categories slowly turn into a public map of a person’s mind.
body: The value of a site like this is cumulative. One entry is just one entry. A hundred entries begin to sketch a worldview.
That is why structure matters: not to look tidy, but to make return possible. Quotes, notes, recommendations, and longer writing should speak to each other.
The longer I work on this kind of archive, the less I want it to feel like a dashboard and the more I want it to feel like a study.

comments.author: Reader
comments.text: The study metaphor is much better than calling it a platform.
